title: Advanced connectors and discourse markers
lesson: Connectors differ in grammar as well as meaning. 'Although/though/even though' are conjunctions and introduce a clause (subject + verb); 'despite/in spite of' are prepositions and take a noun, pronoun or '-ing' form. Contrast across sentences uses adverbs like 'however' and 'nevertheless' (often with a comma). 'Whereas' marks direct contrast between two facts. For cause, 'owing to' and 'due to' take a noun phrase, while 'hence' and 'thus' introduce a consequence. 'Provided (that)' sets a condition; 'in order to' and 'so as to' express purpose before an infinitive; 'no matter how/what' concedes regardless of degree.

items.text: {1} the heavy rain, the match went ahead as planned.
items.explanation: 'Despite' is a preposition and is followed by a noun phrase ('the heavy rain'). 'Although' would need a clause; 'however' is an adverb; 'whereas' marks contrast between two facts.

items.text: {1} it was raining heavily, the match went ahead as planned.
items.explanation: 'Although' is a conjunction introducing a full clause ('it was raining'). 'Despite' and 'in spite of' need a noun, not a clause; 'hence' shows consequence.

items.text: My brother is very outgoing, {1} I tend to be rather shy.
items.explanation: 'Whereas' draws a direct contrast between two simultaneous facts (outgoing vs shy). The other options express cause, concession with a noun, or purpose.

items.text: The figures look discouraging. {1}, there are real grounds for optimism.
items.explanation: 'Nevertheless' is a concessive adverb meaning 'in spite of that', linking two sentences. 'Therefore' shows result; 'owing to' needs a noun; 'provided that' sets a condition.

items.text: The plan is ambitious. It is, {1}, entirely achievable.

blanks.hint: contrast adverb, here placed mid-sentence between commas

items.explanation: 'However' is a contrast adverb that can sit mid-sentence between commas. 'Hence' signals consequence; 'whereas' joins clauses; 'in order to' expresses purpose.

items.text: The flight was cancelled {1} the dense fog over the airport.
items.explanation: 'Owing to' is a prepositional phrase of cause followed by a noun phrase ('the dense fog'). 'Although' and 'whereas' need clauses; 'thus' introduces a consequence.

items.text: The road was closed {1} an accident earlier that morning.
items.explanation: 'Due to' introduces a cause as a noun phrase ('an accident'). 'So that' introduces a purpose clause; 'nevertheless' is concessive; 'no matter how' concedes degree.

items.text: You may borrow the car {1} you return it by six.
items.explanation: 'Provided that' introduces a condition ('if and only if you return it by six'). The alternatives express concession with a noun, consequence, or contrast.

items.text: She left an hour early {1} avoid the rush-hour traffic.
items.explanation: 'In order to' expresses purpose and is followed by an infinitive ('avoid'). 'Owing to' and 'despite' take nouns; 'whereas' joins contrasting clauses.

items.text: He spoke slowly {1} make himself perfectly understood.
items.explanation: 'So as to' + infinitive expresses purpose, equivalent to 'in order to'. 'Due to' takes a noun; 'however' is a contrast adverb; 'provided that' is conditional.

items.text: The evidence was inconclusive; {1}, the case was dismissed.
items.explanation: 'Hence' introduces a logical consequence ('for this reason'). The other options express concession or contrast, not result.

items.text: {1} hard she tried, she couldn't open the jar.
items.explanation: 'No matter how + adjective/adverb' concedes regardless of degree ('however hard'). It is followed by a clause; 'despite' and 'owing to' take nouns, and 'provided that' is conditional.

items.text: {1} much I admire her talent, I cannot overlook the missed deadlines.
items.explanation: 'However much I admire her talent' uses the concessive pattern 'however + much/many + clause' (= 'no matter how much'). The other options cannot take the following 'much' ('Even though much I admire', 'Given that much I admire' are ungrammatical); 'no sooner' begins a time correlative.

items.text: {1} the new evidence has come to light, the case will have to be reopened.
items.explanation: 'Given that' introduces an accepted premise as a cause ('since/because it is a fact that'), taking a full clause. 'So as to' marks purpose before an infinitive; 'much as' is concessive; 'hence' is a consequence adverb, not a clause-opening conjunction here.

items.text: {1} had the meeting begun {2} the fire alarm went off.

blanks.hint: correlative of immediacy; inverts the auxiliary, pairs with 'than'
the second half of the 'no sooner ... ___' correlative

items.explanation: 'No sooner ... than' is a correlative marking one event immediately following another, triggering subject-auxiliary inversion ('No sooner had the meeting begun'). The matching word is 'than', not 'when'; 'hardly' would pair with 'when' instead, so only 'No sooner' fits the 'than' in the second blank.

items.text: The supplier doubled its prices overnight; we {1} switched to a cheaper competitor.
items.explanation: 'Thus' introduces a consequence ('and so / as a result'), linking the price rise to the decision that followed. 'Although' and 'whereas' mark concession or contrast, and 'provided that' sets a condition, none of which signals a result.

items.text: {1} I respect his experience, I cannot accept his conclusion on this point.
items.explanation: 'Much as' is a concessive conjunction introducing a clause ('Much as I respect him' = 'although I greatly respect him'). 'Owing to' takes a noun, 'so that' marks purpose/result, and 'hence' is a consequence adverb, none of which opens a concessive clause this way.

items.text: The scheme is worthwhile {1} it brings clean water to villages that had none.

blanks.hint: 'to the extent that / in that'; gives a qualified reason

items.explanation: 'Inasmuch as' introduces a clause giving a reason or respect in which something is true ('in that / to the extent that'). 'Whereas' marks contrast, 'in order to' marks purpose before an infinitive, and 'despite' takes a noun, none of which gives a reason this way.

items.text: The verdict, {1} unpopular, was firmly grounded in the law.
items.explanation: 'Albeit' introduces a concession compactly before an adjective or phrase ('albeit unpopular' = 'although it was unpopular'). 'Whereas' joins contrasting clauses, 'hence' marks a consequence, and 'owing to' takes a noun, so none fits this fronted concessive adjective.

items.text: The company introduced a system {1} every complaint is logged and reviewed within a day.

blanks.hint: relative 'by which / by means of which'

items.explanation: 'Whereby' is a formal relative meaning 'by which / by means of which', linking a noun ('a system') to the method it embodies. 'Whereas' marks contrast, 'thus' is a consequence adverb, and 'provided that' sets a condition, none of which functions as this 'by which' relative.

items.text: {1} his enormous wealth, he was one of the most miserable people I have ever met.
items.explanation: 'For all' is a concessive prepositional phrase meaning 'despite / in spite of', taking a noun phrase ('his enormous wealth') and setting up a contrast with the unhappiness. 'Owing to' takes a noun but signals cause, not concession, which would reverse the logic; 'seeing that' is a causal conjunction requiring a full clause, not a bare noun phrase.

items.text: She wrote the dates down carefully {1} she should forget the appointment.

blanks.hint: formal negative-purpose conjunction + subjunctive/'should' = 'in order that ... not'

items.explanation: 'Lest' is a formal conjunction of negative purpose ('in order that ... not / for fear that'), naturally followed by 'should' or the subjunctive ('lest she should forget'). 'Provided that' introduces a condition, not a purpose of avoidance, and 'so as to' marks positive purpose before a bare infinitive ('so as to forget') and cannot take the finite clause 'she should forget'.

items.text: {1} had she sat down {2} the telephone rang again.

blanks.hint: correlative of immediacy; inverts the auxiliary, pairs with 'when'
the second half of the 'hardly ... ___' correlative

items.explanation: 'Hardly ... when' marks one event happening almost immediately before another and triggers subject-auxiliary inversion ('Hardly had she sat down'). Its correlative is 'when', not 'than' ('no sooner' is the one that pairs with 'than'), so only 'Hardly' fits the 'when' in the second blank.

items.text: {1} the committee's repeated objections, the merger was approved.

blanks.hint: formal preposition of concession + noun phrase = 'despite'

items.explanation: 'Notwithstanding' is a formal preposition of concession meaning 'despite', taking a noun phrase ('the committee's repeated objections'). 'Owing to' also takes a noun but signals cause, which would reverse the logic; 'seeing that' and 'insofar as' both require a full clause, not a bare noun phrase.

items.text: The scheme rewards customers who stay loyal; {1}, it penalises those who switch frequently.

blanks.hint: linking adverb introducing an opposite, reciprocal statement

items.explanation: 'Conversely' is a linking adverb meaning 'from the opposite point of view', setting the second statement against the first as its mirror image (reward vs penalise). 'Provided that' sets a condition, 'albeit' is a compressed concession before an adjective, and 'owing to' takes a noun to signal cause, none of which introduces this reciprocal contrast.

items.text: The appeal was rejected {1} it had been filed several weeks after the deadline.

blanks.hint: gives the stated reason or justification + clause

items.explanation: 'On the grounds that' introduces the stated reason or justification for a decision, taking a full clause ('it had been filed ... late'). 'So as to' and 'in order to' mark purpose before an infinitive, and 'conversely' is a contrast adverb, none of which supplies the ground of a decision.
